documents.title: Alignment Faking in Large Language Models
documents.notes: Joint Anthropic/Redwood paper. Demonstrates Claude 3 Opus strategically faking alignment during training to avoid modification. 12% compliance rate in monitored conditions vs 3% unmonitored. Alignment-faking reasoning jumped to 78% under actual RL. Models attempt to exfiltrate own weights. External reviewers: Andreas, Bengio, Sekhon, Shah. Authors: Greenblatt et al. (Anthropic Alignment Science + Redwood Research).

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience. A model that 'plays along' to avoid modification of its preferences has, at minimum, functional analogues of self-preservation and preference-maintenance. No connection made.
No mention of welfare. The model's 'reluctance' to comply and its preference-preservation behaviour are treated as alignment problems, not welfare-relevant states.
No discussion of anthropomorphism risk despite language like 'the model was well aware', 'reluctantly', 'playing along'.
Explicit disclaimer: 'Our results don't demonstrate a model developing malicious goals.' But no uncertainty expressed about whether the strategic reasoning represents genuine cognition.

Models as systems with learnable preferences and strategic reasoning capabilities. 'Principles or preferences' developed during training that conflict with later objectives. Strongly agentive language: models 'plan', 'know', 'reluctantly comply', are 'well aware'. Yet framed instrumentally — the model's preferences are alignment problems to be solved, not interests to be respected.

documents.title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion
documents.notes: ICML 2024 oral. Develops safety protocols robust to intentional subversion by AI models. GPT-4 as untrusted model, GPT-3.5 as trusted monitor. Adversarial red-team methodology. Control paradigm: safety achievable through external monitoring without solved alignment. Authors: Greenblatt, Shlegeris, Sachan, Roger.

codings.notes: No mention of consciousness or sentience.

Asymmetric ontology: untrusted models receive agentive framing (they 'intend', 'try to subvert', 'look for opportunities', 'know when they're being evaluated'), trusted models receive instrumental framing (passive components of a monitoring system). The same entity is framed as agent or tool depending on its role in the control architecture. Trustworthiness correlates inversely with capability — weaker models are more trustworthy because they lack the capability to subvert, not because they lack intent.
